title: Sophia Smith Galer’s diary: America, lost in translation
gist: A journalist's DC diary during the US 250th-anniversary celebrations contrasts lavish federal spending on patriotic spectacle (F-35 flyovers, algae cleanup) with chronic underfunding of Native American language revitalization, arguing the Trump administration has both starved indigenous-language programs and actively pushed English-only, renaming policies.

themes.theme: US spending priorities: symbolism over substance

insights.claim: Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act allocated $150m to America's 250th-anniversary celebrations (alongside corporate donations), spent on spectacles like F-35 flyovers and manual algae removal from the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, illustrating a pattern of large discretionary spending on patriotic optics.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Signals continued federal willingness to fund high-visibility nationalist spectacle even amid other budget constraints — a pattern investors tracking US fiscal priorities and defense-adjacent optics spending should note.
insights.quote: Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act last year allocated $150m to be spent on America’s 250th anniversary
insights.locator: My trip to DC was a brief respite

insights.claim: The federal government spent about $17.4m total on Indigenous language revitalization through 2018, versus roughly $2bn (in today's money) historically spent on linguicide efforts such as residential schools that suppressed Native languages — a ratio the author cites as less than 7 cents spent reviving languages for every dollar spent eradicating them.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Quantifies a durable underinvestment gap in indigenous cultural/language programs — relevant for philanthropic, ESG, or cultural-heritage-focused capital looking for underfunded, measurable causes.
insights.quote: for every dollar the U.S. government spent on eradicating Native languages in previous centuries, it spent less than 7 cents on revitalizing them in this one
insights.locator: When researching my book, I came across a 2019 article

insights.claim: The Biden administration's 10-year national plan proposed $16.7bn for Native language revitalization across the continental US, Alaska and Hawaii, but the plan's funding remains unplaced after the administration ended and the second Trump term began.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: A large, still-unallocated federal commitment ($16.7bn) is a policy reversal risk/opportunity to track for anyone monitoring indigenous-affairs or cultural-preservation funding streams.
insights.quote: It called for a $16.7bn investment into revitalisation programmes
insights.locator: This almost changed. The Biden administration released a 10-year national plan

insights.claim: Rather than funding language revitalization, Trump's stated language-related priorities have been declaring English the sole official US language, renaming Denali to Mount McKinley, and renaming the Gulf of Mexico the Gulf of America — symbolic reassertions of Anglo-American naming and identity rather than investment in linguistic diversity.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: A concrete marker of policy direction under the current administration — symbolic nationalist gestures displacing substantive minority-language investment, useful context for cultural-policy and DEI-adjacent risk assessment.

insights.claim: The Karuk Nation in northern California has fewer than 20 remaining speakers of its ancestral language, an example the author cites from her book research into languages under threat of suppression or disappearance.
insights.speaker: Sophia Smith Galer
insights.so_what: Concrete case study of near-extinction-level language loss; a data point for anyone tracking indigenous cultural preservation efforts or documentary/archival opportunities.
insights.locator: Having spent some time interviewing members of the Karuk Nation
